name: El Morredero, Cabeza de Yegua, and Pico Tuerto Trail
name_original: Cabeza de la Yegua, La Mayada y Pico Tuerto – ida y vuelta desde el Puerto de los Portillinos por Montes Aquilianos
description: Hike 8.0 miles (12.8 km) through Montes Aquilianos to Cabeza de la Yegua, La Mayada, and Pico Tuerto, gaining 2119 feet (646 metres) in 4h 5
region_intro: Hiking around Castrillo De Cabrera offers a diverse landscape characterized by ancient Roman canals, protected river valleys, and significant peaks. The region, nestled in the province of León, Spain, features an extensive network of trails that traverse hillsides and connect historical sites. Hikers can explore areas recognized for their ecological importance, such as the Caprada Valley, and ascend peaks like Cabeza de Yegua. This blend of natural beauty and historical elements provides varied terrain for outdoor activities. Best hiking trails around Castrillo De Cabrera The most popular hiking route is Cueva de San Genado from Peñalba de Santiago — round trip, a 3.1 miles (5.0 km) trail that takes 1 hour 40 minutes to complete, featuring moderate elevation gain. Another top favourite among local hikers is El Morredero, Cabeza de Yegua, and Pico Tuerto Trail, a moderate 8.0 miles (12.8 km) path. This route offers views of the Cabeza de Yegua peak, the second-highest in the Montes de León. Local hikers also love the La Mirada Circular Natural Trail, a 3.5 miles (5.7 km) trail leading through varied natural scenery, often completed in about 1 hour 55 minutes. Hiking around Castrillo De Cabrera is defined by ancient Roman canals, the protected Caprada Valley, and the challenging Cabeza de Yegua Peak. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to difficult ascents. The routes in Castrillo De Cabrera are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 30 reviews. More than 280 hikers have used komoot to explore Castrillo De Cabrera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Castrillo De Cabrera?
A: Castrillo De Cabrera offers a wide selection of hiking opportunities, with over 20 trails to explore. These range from easy walks to more challenging ascents, catering to various skill levels.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Castrillo De Cabrera?
A: The trails in Castrillo De Cabrera are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an average rating of 4.7 stars from over 30 reviews. More than 280 hikers have already used komoot to discover the diverse terrain here, often praising the unique blend of natural beauty and historical elements.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Castrillo De Cabrera has easy trails perfect for beginners or families. One excellent option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1336445541/la-mirada-circular-natural-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Mirada Circular Natural Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which is 3.5 miles (5.7 km) long and leads through varied natural scenery, typically completed in under two hours.

Q: Can I explore historical sites while hiking in Castrillo De Cabrera?
A: Absolutely. The region is rich in history. You can hike along impressive ancient Roman canals, such as the C3 and C4, which connect various villages. Trails also lead past fascinating historical elements like ancient mills, pre-Roman settlements (castros) like Castro La Cárcava, and the restored forge (La Fragua Restaurada), offering a unique blend of nature and heritage.

Q: Are there any waterfalls or scenic viewpoints to discover?
A: Yes, Castrillo De Cabrera offers several scenic spots. You can find the tranquil &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4719916" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Silence Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy panoramic vistas from viewpoints like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5487496" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Los Doce Apóstoles&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5518531" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Valle del Arroyo de la Yegua&lt;/a&gt;. For challenging climbs, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5518524" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Tuerto (2,051 m)&lt;/a&gt; also offers breathtaking views.

Q: What are the natural highlights of the region for hikers?
A: The natural highlights include the ecologically significant Caprada Valley, a Site of Community Importance and Special Protection Area for Birds, through which the Cabo River flows. For more challenging adventures, the Cabeza de Yegua Peak, the second-highest in the Montes de León, offers demanding climbs and extensive views. You can also find tranquil spots like 'Las Pozas de Lino' for relaxation amidst nature.

Q: Are there challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers, there are several challenging routes. One notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1336381252/penalba-de-santiago-from-montes-de-valdueza-circular-by-the-source-of-the-rio-oza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñalba de Santiago from Montes de Valdueza — circular by the source of the rio Oza&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 10.8 miles (17.4 km) trail with significant elevation gain, offering a rewarding experience.

Q: Are there circular hiking routes in Castrillo De Cabrera?
A: Yes, the region features several circular hiking routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1336463161/cueva-de-san-genado-from-penalba-de-santiago-round-trip" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva de San Genado from Peñalba de Santiago — round trip&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 3.1 miles (5.0 km) circular trail that takes about 1 hour 40 minutes to complete.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Castrillo De Cabrera?
A: Many trails in natural areas like Castrillo De Cabrera are generally dog-friendly, but it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in protected areas like the Caprada Valley, which is a Special Protection Area for Birds. Always check local signage for specific restrictions or requirements.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Castrillo De Cabrera?
A: The best time to hike in Castrillo De Cabrera generally depends on your preferred conditions. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, ideal for most hikers. Summer can be warm, especially at lower elevations, while winter can bring snow to higher peaks like Cabeza de Yegua, offering opportunities for snowshoeing or more challenging winter hikes.

Q: Is parking available near the trailheads in Castrillo De Cabrera?
A: While specific parking information for every trailhead isn't always available, villages in the Castrillo De Cabrera municipality typically offer public parking areas. It's advisable to check maps or local information for designated parking spots near your chosen starting point, especially for popular routes.

Q: Are there less crowded hiking options for a peaceful experience?
A: Given the extensive network of trails, including those along the 110 kilometers of Roman canals, it is possible to find less crowded options. Exploring routes that delve into the more remote sections of the Roman canals or lesser-known paths in the Caprada Valley can offer a peaceful and solitary hiking experience away from more popular spots.
tips: Located at an altitude of 1,999 meters on an unnamed hill, we have impressive views of the Valle del Arroyo de la Yegua.
Beautiful path that we find at the Silla de la Yegua pass with fantastic views of Alto de las Berdiaínas and Cabeza de la Yegua.

Pico Tuerto is a mountain located in the Montes Aquilianos, in the Bierzo region, offering spectacular views of the region, including Peñalba de Santiago and Ponferrada.

It is a popular hiking destination, and one of the most popular routes begins at the Los Portillinos mountain pass. From there, you can ascend past other peaks such as Cabeza de la Yegua and Pico Morredero.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this hike?
faqs.answer: This hike is an out-and-back route that typically takes around 4 hours and 55 minutes to complete at a steady pace. However, allow extra time for enjoying the stunning panoramic views and taking photos, which could extend your trip to 6 hours or more.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this trail?
faqs.answer: This trail is considered to have a moderate to hard difficulty level. It's best suited for hikers with good physical fitness due to its length, elevation gain, and varied terrain, including some steep sections and parts that may be less clearly marked.

faqs.question: Where can I park at the trailhead?
faqs.answer: The trail starts from Puerto de los Portillinos. You can find parking available at or near this starting point, which is a common access point for hikes in the Montes Aquilianos.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs are generally welcome on trails in the Montes Aquilianos. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially since the area is home to flocks of sheep and goats guarded by mastiffs, particularly on the north face of the mountains.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to hike this route is typically during the late spring, summer, and early autumn months. During these periods, the weather is generally more favorable, and you can fully appreciate the diverse flora and panoramic views. Winter can bring snow and more challenging conditions.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay an entrance fee for this hike?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay an entrance fee to hike this specific trail in the Montes Aquilianos. The area is freely accessible for hikers.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain should I expect on the trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied. You'll encounter wide tracks or forest trails, sections along a ridge, and about 200 meters across open terrain (campo a través). Some parts, especially descents from the ridge, might be un-signposted and partially obscured by heather, requiring good navigation skills. The ascent to Cabeza de la Yegua involves a significant slope on a wide path.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest or viewpoints along the way?
faqs.answer: This route offers spectacular panoramic views. Key highlights include the View of Valle del Arroyo de la Yegua, passing through Collado de la Silla de la Yegua, and reaching the summit of Pico Tuerto (2,051 m). From Cabeza de la Yegua, you can see Pico Teleno, the Valle del Silencio, and even the city of Ponferrada.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with parts of the official trails: [CIMA LE13] El Morredero - Llano de las Ovejas * Peñalba de Santiago and [CIMA LE13] El Morredero - Llano de las Ovejas * San Cristóbal de Valdueza.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like restrooms or water sources along the trail?
faqs.answer: This is a natural mountain trail, and as such, there are no dedicated restrooms or official water sources directly along the route. It's crucial to carry enough water for your entire hike and plan for your needs accordingly. The starting point at Puerto de los Portillinos is a good place to prepare.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate to hard difficulty and varied terrain, you should bring sturdy hiking boots, sufficient water, snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and layers of clothing suitable for changing mountain weather. A map, compass, or GPS device is also highly recommended due to some less-marked sections.
